trajectory_summary: The call starts with a cooperative customer who has a practical question, gets a reassuring answer, and is willing to proceed. The rep completes basic verify items efficiently but never builds energy — no bill-pain anchor, no curiosity created, no emotional engagement with the mother's death disclosure. The call ends with a soft callback ('call you back whenever') rather than a conditional booking, leaving the outcome entirely dependent on the customer's initiative to send a bill photo. The trajectory is flat from the moment the bill-access problem surfaces and never recovers.
outcome_dispute: v1 outcome tag is unknown. Based on the transcript, the correct classification is 'soft callback / no appointment' — not a booked sit. The rep sent a text and said 'I'll call you back whenever,' which is a callback with no locked time. Per codex analysis_directives.soft_outcome_distinction, this is materially different from a locked callback and carries high no-show risk. If v1 tagged this as 'booked,' that is a miscall.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Conditional booking structure — converting soft callbacks into locked next steps
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I sent you a text, and then you can just send a picture of that back. And then I'll call you back whenever, and then I'll schedule you. Okay?
primary_coaching_focus.why: This call had a cooperative customer, a brand-new roof, a verbal bill estimate, and a clear path to a sit — and it ended with 'call you back whenever.' The rep needs one move: when the bill can't be captured live, lock a specific callback time as a conditional booking. 'Once I get the bill photo, I'll call you Thursday at 10 — does that work?' That's the difference between a sit and a ghost. On a paid lead, this is a paycheck-protection skill, not a nice-to-have.

key_moments.quote: when my mom passed away, they Oh, Oh, I'm sorry. Different family members.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is speaker_0 (the customer) disclosing her mother's recent death mid-search, then self-interrupting with her own 'Oh, I'm sorry.' The rep's nearest audible response is the fragment 'It's' at 06:21 — unintelligible, and almost certainly not an empathy acknowledgment. The rep has no audible utterance at the moment of disclosure. This is a more severe gap than wrong-execution: the disclosure received no response at all. The customer self-recovered and continued searching, which means the rep was spared the visible consequence — but the trust cost is real.

key_moments.quote: I sent you a text, and then you can just send a picture of that back. And then I'll call you back whenever, and then I'll schedule you. Okay?
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's structural failure. The rep exits with a soft callback — 'call you back whenever' — rather than a conditional booking with a locked time. None of the button_up required elements are present: no appointment datetime confirmed, no one-hour block stated, no phone appointment specified, no no-driving/at-computer anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The outcome is entirely dependent on the customer sending a bill photo and the rep remembering to follow up.

key_moments.quote: Okay. As long as you all own it and you're all in agreeance and you, own the roof as well, you're fine.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep correctly identifies three co-owners on title (Ernest and two sisters) but does not flag the appointment-inclusion requirement. Per the codex, co-owners on title must be present at the appointment — the same logic as spouse_decision applies here. The rep acknowledges the three-owner structure and moves on without establishing that all three (or at minimum Ernest, who holds the bill and is the primary occupant) need to be on the call.

key_moments.quote: His mailbox is full, and he's not answering.
key_moments.stewart_read: Ernest is unreachable and holds the bill. The rep's response is to ask the customer to search for a physical paper bill — which burns several minutes and fails. The codex's landline_protocol and alternative_paths section covers this scenario: when the bill-holder is absent and the customer cannot produce the bill live, the correct pivot is either verbal readback (the customer's husband had already provided an estimate of ~$140) or deferring bill collection to the specialist. The rep had a verbal estimate in hand at 04:16 ('he said, was around about 140') but did not use it as a bridge to schedule.
